observations.recommendation:
The Audit Team recommended that the LCE direct:
The ME to provide timely report on the cost and status of on-going projects and submit detailed cost of components of the said projects for proper recording in the books and facilitate the transfer of these projects from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts and Registry of Public Infrastructures upon completion in compliance with Section 4.6 of COA Circular No. 2016-004.
The MA for the reclassification from CIP to the appropriate PPE accounts the 22 completed projects amounting to ₱235,194,497.31 and reconcile the difference of ₱3,473,942.81 between his ledger/ records and the report of the Engineering Office in order to ensure the completeness, accuracy, and reliability of the FS.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the:
GSO Designate to conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties, determine which items costing below ₱50,000.00 which are still in storage or already issued to end-users, reclassify these as Semi-Expendable or Expense items in accordance with COA Circular No. 2024-006, and prepare and submit the RPCPPE and the RPCSEP to reflect the proper classification of assets.
MA to apply the appropriate accounting treatment based on the GSO’s report, reclassify or expense the properties below the capitalization threshold, and ensure the fair presentation of the FS by correcting the overstatement of PPE. accumulated depreciation and depreciation accounts and the understatement of Semi-Expendable and/or Expense accounts.

For the proper management and monitoring of receivables, the Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the Acting MT and the implementing offices or designated focal persons to promptly send notices or demand letters to all concerned parties, including NGOs/POs, individuals, and other entities responsible for outstanding balances, requiring the immediate submission of liquidation reports and/or collection of receivables, and to adopt other recovery measures, including legal remedies when necessary, prior to derecognition, to ensure the LGUs recovers funds to finance development projects.
Direct the MA to conduct regular reconciliation and verification of all receivable’s accounts, including Accounts Receivable, Due from Officers and Employees, Due from LGUs/NGAs, Loan Receivables, and Other Receivables, to promptly identify and correct errors such as the negative balances, duplicated payments, and uncollectible amounts.
Require the MA to avail of the advantages of COA Circular No. 2023-008 and follow the procedures for proper disposition and derecognition of dormant accounts as mandated by Sections 7 and 8 of the Circular to fairly present the financial statements.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCA instruct the MA to prepare adjusting journal entry by debiting Prior Period Adjustment and crediting Accumulated Depreciation – Road Networks amounting to ₱2,569,170.79 to restate the correct balance of the PPE accounts for CY 2024.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE and the District Supervisor / Co-chairman of the LSB, to charge only the allowable expenditures or programs/projects/activities under SEF in compliance with Section 272 of RA 7160, Section 4 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017, and Section 2 of Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020 in order to ensure utmost delivery of basic education services and avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to prioritize the conduct of periodic, thorough and complete physical count of PPE, including infrastructure assets, and invite the Audit Team or his representative to witness the activity, and ensure prompt submission of duly and completely accomplished inventory reports within the prescribed periods in order to ascertain the correctness, completeness, existence and physical condition of all properties presented in the FS. Likewise, we recommended that Management avail of the advantages of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts by following the guidelines and procedures prescribed in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to ascertain its accuracy and fair presentation in the FS.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Acting MT to update, balance, rule, foot and accomplish the certification at the end of every month the cashbooks maintained in compliance with Section 26 to 28, Volume II of the NGAS Manual for LGUs and Section 111 (1) of PD No. 1445 in order to readily ascertain the total accountability for a given period and allow the immediate reconciliation of balances between the ledger and cashbook.
Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Acting MT and MA to determine the accountability of the present and previous designated MTs and prepare the necessary accounting entries in order to recognize in the books the appropriate receivable accounts and correct cash balances that corresponds to the period of their incumbency in order to fairly present the Cash - Local Treasury account in the FS.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
Direct the BAC and the Acting ME to review all infrastructure project contracts and closely monitor their implementation to promptly address delays and impose liquidated damages or terminate contracts, as warranted, under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.
Enforce administrative sanctions against erring contractors, if warranted, to protect government interests and prevent recurring delays in project implementation.
consider terminating contracts with non-performing contractors, if justified, and re-award the remaining works to capable contractors to ensure project completion and delivery of intended benefits to constituents.
Instruct the Acting ME and the MA to regularly monitor and prepare reports on project status, percentage of completion, and payments made to contractors.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the LCE direct the Acting MT to remit the remaining outstanding balance of ₱172,295.44 to the Pag-IBIG Fund, ensure the complete and timely remittance of all withheld Pag-IBIG contributions and loan amortizations, and see to it that the disbursement vouchers for the remittances will be transmitted to the MA for proper recording in the books.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Acting MT and MA to immediately settle the unpaid PhilHealth contributions totaling ₱81,661.88 and see to it that contributions are remitted in full thereby safeguarding employees’ benefit entitlements and ensuring the Municipality’s compliance with the law.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) to prioritize and develop PPAs that are fully prepared for procurement and implementation to ensure timely and effective use of the 20% Development Fund; and
Require the MPDO to expedite the implementation of PPAs funded by unutilized continuing appropriations. For projects that are no longer priorities, have no pending obligations, or represent savings from completed activities, instruct the proposal of fund reprogramming to the Sanggunian for allocation to other urgent and eligible development programs. This will enhance fund utilization and promote the socio-economic welfare of the constituents.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the LDRRMO to prepare LDRRMFIP to support any expenditures or disbursement made from the LDRRMF and require the MA to revert the unutilized LDRRMF after five years to the unappropriated surplus of the GF thus, available funds could be used for other social services after subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the MA and the LDRRMO to prepare and submit respectively, the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and submit to the Office of the Auditor to comply with COA Circular No. 2012-002 in order to facilitate timely review, verification and monitoring of the utilization of the said fund.

We recommended that the LCE:

Require the MA to submit the 2023 Year-End Trial Balances and the complete set of FS.

Remind the MA to prepare the required monthly Pre-Closing Trial Balances and other reports which will result to easier and immediate preparation of the year-end trial balances and FS in order to meet the set deadline of submission; and

Instruct the MA and Acting MT to install monitoring and control systems over the movement of financial documents. This is to avoid the source documents being misplaced or lost thus, enable timely recording of transactions and immediate preparation and submission of the year-end trial balances and FS within the set deadline as prescribed in Section 41 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The MA already submitted the 2023 Year-End Trial Balances and the complete set of FS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Inventory and Appraisal Committee of the municipality conduct further verification to reconcile the balance of the Property, Plant and Equipment account as presented in the financial statements with the balance in the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for fair presentation of the balances of PPE accounts. In addition, transactions shall be posted promptly from source documents and any discrepancies noted in Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) with the physical inventory shall be immediately verified and adjusted.

We recommended that Municipal Government:
1. Conduct further verification on the material difference of cash and receivable accounts as against the balances of the liabilities and continuing appropriations to avoid the occurrence of cash overdraft.
2.  Avoid deficit spending because overdraft is not a good fiscal administration to go beyond one’s financial capacity to shoulder and it is prohibited by law.
3.  Apply effective cash programming to facilitate the proper handling of cash.

We recommended that Municipal Government:
Through the Municipal Accountant require all the necessary documents for the payment of financial assistance from the NGO/PO before payment shall be made.
Evaluate properly the Memorandum of Agreement if it covers the pertinent terms and reference most advantageous to the beneficiaries considering the cost effectiveness of the project.
Issue crossed check under the name of the NGO/PO to ascertain that the financial assistance will be deposited in the account of the NGO/PO.
Strictly identify the priority projects if it is beyond their capability to execute such project before granting the award to the NGO/PO for implementation, which shall be clearly defined in the MOA. Extending a financial assistance to NGO/PO wherein the municipal government is capable of implementing the project constitute an unnecessary expenditure.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant use the prescribed account titles as set forth by COA Circular No. 2015-009. Compliance to the provisions of the said circular would result to fair presentation of the related items in the financial statements.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant prepare and submit Bank Reconciliation Statements in compliance with existing rules and regulations. The preparation of such would increase the reliability of the Cash in Bank account resulting to fair presentation of the items presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that:
Management formulate and develop programs for the utilization of its shares from RA 8240 that are consistent with the objectives set forth by Section 3.2 of DBM Local Budget Memorandum Circular No. 76 dated October 6, 2017;
Management consider the provisions of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007 in granting financial assistance to NGOs/POs; and
Management should adhere to all laws and regulations applicable to Advances for Officers and Employees, Donations and financial assistance to NGOs/POs, otherwise expenditures incurred without adhering to established laws, rules and regulations are considered irregular expenditures.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant prepare immediately the disbursement vouchers covering such remittances and supporting statements to the Local Government Units (LGUs) to avoid possible misappropriation/misuse of the funds.

We recommended that Acting Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections daily or not later than the next banking day as provided by the above-stated accounting rules and regulations to avoid possible loss or misappropriation of government funds.

We recommended that Municipality acquire insurance policies for all its insurable properties with the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to provide an indemnification scheme for the agency in case of losses arising from fortuitous events.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant submit to the Auditor copy of the Registry of Public Infrastructure (RPI) as maintained by the municipality which will serve as a basis in evaluating the municipality’s extent of compliance to the transitional provision of IPSAS 17 as stated in Section 3.7 of COA Circular No. 2016-004. Adjustments, if necessary, are to be made in the 2019 books of accounts to update the required threshold of 100% as at December 31, 2019.

We recommend that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

We recommended that Management observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the liquidation of cash advances.  Accountable Officers should take note of the following sanctions that can be instituted upon them as a consequence of their inability to settle their cash advances as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012, to wit:
Filing of malversation charge;
Shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of salaries of erring accountable officers; and
Institution of appropriate administrative proceedings.

We recommend that Municipal Accountant transfer the unexpended balances of the QRF and MF-MOOE to the account “Trust Liability-DRRM” in the Trust Fund Books as provided for in the COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
We recommend that Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary documents to facilitate the immediate return of the unspent PDAF to the National Treasury.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant use the prescribed account title in the recognition and recording of the municipality’s shares from the Lotto Charity Fund of PCSO as set forth by COA Circular No. 2015-009. Compliance to the provisions of the said circular would result in fair presentation of the related items in the financial statements. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary correcting entries for the accounts affected for their fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management submit prior approval from the Bureau of Treasury to open and maintain an account in bank other than those listed in Section 5.2, otherwise effect immediately the closure of the bank account at Philippine National Bank-Narvacan Branch and transfer the existing account balance to any Authorized Government Depository Banks (AGDBs) as enumerated by DOF Circular No. 01-2017 dated May 11, 2017.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant check the completeness of the supporting documents required for the payment of terminal leave benefits and monetization of leave credits as set forth by COA Circular 2012-001 to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended that municipality formulate its Annual Procurement Plan (APP) based on the different programs, activities and projects that are crucial to the effective discharge of its governmental function or are in pursuit of its principal mandate. All procurement of supplies needed for the day-to-day operations of the municipality shall be undertaken in accordance with the approved APP.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer issue Official Receipts under Trust Fund account of the municipality for the proceeds from sale of bid documents to ensure that such collections are treated as trust receipts to be exclusively used for the payment of honoraria and overtime pay of BAC personnel of the municipality. By doing so, the transactions are also ensured to be accounted for properly in books of accounts by the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that Management release the RPT shares of the Barangays within the prescribed period set forth by Section 271 (d) of Republic Act No. 7160 to provide additional funds of the Barangays for the implementation of programs/projects/activities that would benefit their constituents.

We recommended that in the payment of overtime services to government employees, provisions of CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2015 dated November 25, 2015 must be strictly observed. The Agency Head shall be held responsible for the proper implementation of the provisions of the above Joint Circular; any unauthorized or excess payments shall be refunded by the employees concerned.

We recommended that management grant special cash advances only to duly designated and properly bonded Disbursing Officers or Accountable Officers. The Accountable Officer shall prepare liquidation report with complete supporting documents and submit the same to the Accountant within the prescribed period as required in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
We recommended that management grant clothing allowances to LGU personnel only if the required length of service as set forth under DBM Budget Circular No. 2018-1 dated March 8, 2018 is met.

We recommended that management submit legal basis to justify the payment of Cash Gift to two hundred four (204) Job Order Employees of the Municipal Government, otherwise such payment shall be refunded. Likewise, we encourage management to discontinue payment of personnel benefits which is inconsistent with existing rules, regulations, procedural guidelines, policies, principles or practices that have gained recognition in laws.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant reconcile the difference of P165,884.98 between the ledger balance of the Loan Payable-Domestic account and the outstanding balance as confirmed from Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP)-Narvacan Branch. The outstanding balance of Loan Payable account as confirmed by a bank or any lending institution should tally with the amount appearing in the financial statements to achieve fair presentation.

We recommended that management, through the Municipal Accountant, ensure that these documents are complete before any payment shall be made to avoid doubt on the validity and propriety of transactions. Also, the BAC shall require the suppliers/contactors to submit all the documentary requirements before recommending to the HOPE the award of contract.

With the various deficiencies noted above, we recommend that:
The Municipal Mayor designate a Petty Cash Custodian to delineate the function of the Acting Municipal Treasurer;
The granting of cash advances for Petty Operating Expenses shall be supported with copy of authority by the Agency Head for initial cash advance and estimate of expenses. Only unexpended balance at the end of each year shall be refunded to Cashier/Collecting Officer; and
The Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 4.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and replenishment of PCF shall be equal to the total amount of expenditures made therefrom.

We recommended that management submit legal basis to justify the payments of honorarium to a non-government personnel/a private individual sitting as MFARM Chairman as to its legality, regularity and propriety, otherwise such payments shall be refunded.

For the foregoing deficiencies noted, we recommended that:
The Municipality strictly comply with the disbursement procedure for the Special Education Fund. Proper certification by the District Supervisor on Special Education Fund disbursements should be observed;
The Municipality strictly observe the allowable expenses enumerated in DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 in the disbursement of Special Education Fund. The Local School Board (LSB) shall formulate  plan/program indicating strategic prioritization policies in the allocation of SEF to schools; and
The  Municipal Accountant properly classify the items of expenditures charged under the Special Education Fund (SEF) that would result to fair presentation of the items presented in the financial statements and require the necessary documents based on the nature of the transaction.

We recommended management through the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the above cited provision. Submit on time all the necessary reports together with their complete supporting documents in order not to delay the post audit of transactions and preparation of other reports. It is the direct responsibility of the agency head to install, implement, and monitor sound internal control.

We recommended that the establishment of the Local Council for Protection of Children be headed by the Municipal Mayor. In consonance to this is the appropriation in the Municipal Government’s Annual Budget one percent (1%) of its IRA as LCPC fund for programs, projects and activities for the protection of children.

We recommended that Management, particularly the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), regularly procure common-use supplies, materials and equipment which are necessary in its operations from the Procurement Service-Department of Budget and Management (PS-DBM) as provided under Administrative Order No. 17 to contribute to the attainment of a modernized and standardized government procurement system and to avail of the most economic price in the acquisition of supplies and materials.

We recommended that Management maintain separate depository accounts for each fund to facilitate proper recording of transactions, timely monitoring of available balances of each fund, and to simplify the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended that Municipal Treasurer and/or the Municipal Accountant remit immediately the amount due to the BIR pursuant to Revenue Memorandum Circular 23-2007 dated March 20, 2007 in order not to deprive the National Government the use of its resources. The person in-charge of withholding shall prepare the appropriate forms, file the tax returns to the BIR collecting agents and pay the corresponding withholding taxes due thereon.

We recommended that a special cash advance shall be drawn by the Municipal Treasurer for the payment of cash prizes for the various activities in the celebration of the Annual Town Fiesta. Payment through reimbursement basis should be strictly stopped to avoid possible misuse of funds.
observations.reason: Cash Prizes for various activities during the celebration of 2018 Town Fiesta were already made through cash advance by the Acting Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Budget Officer and the Acting Municipal Accountant, refrain from processing the payment of unobligated prior year’s expenditures in the current year. The municipality should provide mechanism to ensure that all lawful expenditures and obligation incurred during a fiscal year shall be taken up in the accounts of that year to avoid disallowance in audit. Further, the account “Prior period adjustment” should be accordingly used as prescribed by COA Circular 2015-009.
observations.reason: The account “Prior Period Adjustment” was no longer used for cash transactions of the municipality pertaining to prior year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that a sound internal control should be in place in order to ensure that financial transactions are properly authorized, executed and recorded and segregation of duties is its key concept. To address the issue on incompatible duties being performed by the municipal treasurer-ICO, we further recommended that the Municipal Engineer be the Immediate Supervisor of all the labor-related works of the municipality.
observations.reason: Various Labor Payrolls prepared by the Municipality were already signed by the Public Services Officer (PSO) III serving as the Immediate Supervisor of the laborers. The Acting Municipal Treasurer now only acted as the Disbursing Officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned municipal personnel observe the complete and proper accomplishment of Driver’s Trip Tickets and the submission of Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 to provide basis in determining the reasonableness of fuel consumption of each municipal vehicle.
observations.reason: Trip Tickets attached to the DVs are already properly accomplished and serially numbered. The Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption is being submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the municipal employees to strictly adhere to the provisions of the above-cited rules and regulations. A memorandum should be accordingly issued as to the preparation of the Itinerary of Travels properly indicating therein the time of departure and arrival which will serve as the basis in the grant of the allowed travel expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office to closely coordinate with the HRMD to ensure that withholding taxes in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table are deducted from honorarium in accordance with the above-mentioned regulations and the same are remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Taxes were already withheld for 2018 transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipality promptly remit the withheld taxes and compulsory premium contributions to BIR and PhilHealth, respectively, on the period stipulated by the above-mentioned laws and regulations to avoid incurrence of penalties. In addition, adjusting entry should be prepared to re-establish the amounts debited to the liability accounts pertaining to the penalties incurred and paid. Further, penalties incurred that were paid from the funds of the municipality are subject to disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: During 2018, the municipality has exerted their efforts to remit the mandatory deductions to respective agencies to avoid incurrence of penalties such as surcharges, interest and compromise fee. Though there was penalties incurred in 2018, the amount has considerably decreased compared to the amount incurred in 2017.
observations.reason: Notice of Disallowances (NDs), Notice of Finality of Decisions (NFDs), and COA Order of Executions (COEs) were already issued by the audit team. Penalties were still noted for some 2018 transactions and will be accordingly issued with audit disallowances.

observations.recommendation:
Advance payments were made to contractors despite the non-submission to and non-acceptance by the procuring entity of an irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee, or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by surety or insurance Company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the procuring entity.

Retention money was released before final acceptance by the procuring entity to the contractors despite the absence of any security in the form of cash, bank guarantee, or irrevocable standby letter of credit from a commercial bank, GSIS or surety bond callable on demand.

The Construction Safety and Health Program submitted by contractors of infrastructure projects were not approved by the Department of Labor and Employment and the cost of implementing the same was not included in the Approved Budget for the Contract contrary to existing laws, rules and regulations thus, compliance of the contractor to the required safety measures in the worksite is not guaranteed.

The Bids and Awards Committee was not able to invite observers in all stages of their procurement process which is not in consonance to Section 13 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act 9184 thus the objective of transparency was not achieved.

For the foregoing deficiencies noted, we recommended the following:

The advance payment shall be made only upon the submission to and acceptance by the procuring entity of an irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee, or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by surety or insurance Company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the procuring entity,

The total retention money shall be due for release upon acceptance of the works and upon the submission by the contractor of irrevocable standby letters of credit from a commercial bank, bank guarantees or surety bonds callable on demand, of amounts equivalent to the retention money; 

The Construction Safety and Health Program (CSHP) shall be duly approved by the Department of Labor and Employment, and the related cost of implementing  such program be incorporated in the preparation of the Approved Budget for the Contract. Eventually, every contractor should be required to comply with the implementation of the same; and   

The Bids and Awards Committee invite observers in all stages of their procurement process in accordance with Section 13 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 to promote transparency in the procurement process of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality, through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), has intensified its efforts in strictly adhering to provisions of R.A. 9184. Seminars attended by BAC members enabled them to acquire additional knowledge as to the technical aspects of procurements, particularly in the preparation of necessary documents required in the auditorial review of contracts. The Municipality’s compliance with the previous audit observations were manifested in the procurements of various goods and infrastructure projects in 2018.
observations.reason: Some infractions were already implemented by the agency such as the required documents for the advance payment to contractors and for the release of retention money. Invitations to Observers were already made by the agency for most of its 2018 procurements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant prepare immediately the disbursement vouchers for the payment of the unremitted trust collections due to the Provincial Government to avoid misappropriation/misuse of the funds.

We recommended that a special cash advance shall be drawn by the Municipal Treasurer for the payment of cash prizes for the various activities in the celebration of the Annual Town Fiesta. Payment through reimbursement basis should be strictly stopped to avoid possible misuse of funds.

We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Budget Officer and the Acting Municipal Accountant, refrain from processing the payment of unobligated prior year’s expenditures in the current year. The municipality should provide mechanism to ensure that all lawful expenditures and obligation incurred during a fiscal year shall be taken up in the accounts of that year to avoid disallowance in audit. Further, the account “Prior period adjustment” should be accordingly used as prescribed by COA Circular 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipality observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the liquidation of cash advances.  Accountable Officers should take note of the following sanctions that can be instituted upon them as a consequence of their inability to settle their cash advances as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012, to wit:
Filing of malversation charge;
Suspension of salaries of erring accountable officers; and
Institution of appropriate administrative proceedings.

With the various deficiencies noted above, we recommended the following:
The Acting Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 48 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs by adopting the imprest system in accounting for petty cash fund,
The Municipal Mayor to designate a Petty Cash Custodian to delineate the function of the Acting Municipal Treasurer, and
The Sangguniang Bayan to prepare a resolution approving the estimates of petty cash expenses for one month as prepared by the Accounting Office. Such resolution shall be used in the setting up of the petty cash fund at the beginning of the year.
We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections daily or not later than the next banking day as provided by the above stated accounting rules and regulations.
We recommended that the remaining PDAF with the municipality be returned to the National Treasury.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant prepare and submit Bank Reconciliation Statements in compliance with existing rules and regulations. The preparation of such would increase the reliability of the Cash in Bank account resulting to fair presentation of the items presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that a sound internal control should be in place in order to ensure that financial transactions are properly authorized, executed and recorded and segregation of duties is its key concept. To address the issue on incompatible duties being performed by the municipal treasurer-ICO, we further recommended that the Municipal Engineer be the Immediate Supervisor of all the labor-related works of the municipality.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant submit to the Auditor copy of the Registry of Public Infrastructure (RPI) as maintained by the municipality which will serve as a basis in evaluating the municipality’s extent of compliance to the transitional provision of IPSAS 17. Adjustments, if necessary, are to be made in the 2017 books of accounts to update the required threshold of 50% as at December 31, 2017.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to transfer the unexpended balances of the QRF and MF-MOOE to the appropriate account, “Trust Liability-DRRM”, in the Trust Fund Books as provided for in the COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant see to it that documents to support financial assistance granted to NGOs/POs are complete and in accordance with COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007 before certifying in the disbursement voucher on the completeness of the supporting documents to avoid suspension in post audit.

We recommended that the concerned municipal personnel observe the complete and proper accomplishment of Driver’s Trip Tickets and the submission of Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 to provide basis in determining the reasonableness of fuel consumption of each municipal vehicle.

We recommended that the municipality through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit copies of perfected contracts and all its supporting documents to the Auditor within the period prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Government maintain separate depository accounts for each fund to facilitate monitoring of available balances and avoid incurrence of unnecessary bank charges and to simplify the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended that the municipality intensify the preparation of its Annual Procurement Plan (APP) by extensively determining the procurement projects that are crucial to the effective discharge of its governmental function or are in pursuit of its principal mandate. By doing so, procurements of supplies as needed in the daily operation of the municipality can be made on a cost-efficient and timely manner and not as the need arises.
We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant see to it that payments of terminal leave benefits should be supported with complete documentation to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor effect immediately the closure of the bank account at Philippine National Bank-Narvacan Branch and transfer the existing account balance to any Authorized Government Depository Banks (AGDBs) as enumerated by DOF Circular No. 01-2017 dated May 11, 2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the municipal employees to strictly adhere to the provisions of the above-cited rules and regulations. A memorandum should be accordingly issued as to the preparation of the Itinerary of Travels properly indicating therein the time of departure and arrival which will serve as the basis in the grant of the allowed travel expenses.

We recommended that the Municipality acquire insurance policies for all its insurable properties with the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to provide an indemnification scheme for the agency in case of losses arising from fortuitous events.

We recommended that the municipality strictly comply with the disbursement procedure for the Special Education Fund. Proper certification by the District Supervisor on Special Education Fund disbursements should be observed.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer remit immediately the amount due pursuant to BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 to avoid incurrence of penalties. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary documents to facilitate the immediate remittance of the unremitted taxes.

We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office to closely coordinate with the HRMD to ensure that withholding taxes in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table are deducted from honorarium in accordance with the above-mentioned regulations and the same are remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the municipality promptly remit the withheld taxes and compulsory premium contributions to BIR and PhilHealth, respectively, on the period stipulated by the above-mentioned laws and regulations to avoid incurrence of penalties. In addition, adjusting entry should be prepared to re-establish the amounts debited to the liability accounts pertaining to the penalties incurred and paid. Further, penalties incurred that were paid from the funds of the municipality are subject to disallowance in audit.

For the foregoing deficiencies noted, we recommended the following:
The advance payment shall be made only upon the submission to and acceptance by the procuring entity of an irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee, or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by surety or insurance Company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the procuring entity,
The total retention money shall be due for release upon acceptance of the works and upon the submission by the contractor of irrevocable standby letters of credit from a commercial bank, bank guarantees or surety bonds callable on demand, of amounts equivalent to the retention money;
The Construction Safety and Health Program (CSHP) shall be duly approved by the Department of Labor and Employment, and the related cost of implementing  such program be incorporated in the preparation of the Approved Budget for the Contract. Eventually, every contractor should be required to comply with the implementation of the same; and
The Bids and Awards Committee invite observers in all stages of their procurement process in accordance with Section 13 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 to promote transparency in the procurement process of the municipality.

We recommended that the Management, particularly the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), regularly procure common-use supplies, materials and equipment which are necessary in its operations from the Procurement Service-Department of Budget and Management (PS-DBM) as provided under Administrative Order No. 17 to contribute to the attainment of a modernized and standardized government procurement system and to avail of the most economic price in the acquisition of supplies and materials.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer remit immediately the amount due for contributions withheld pursuant to existing government rules and regulations to avoid incurrence of penalties. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary documents to facilitate the immediate remittance of the withheld contributions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain special accounts under the GF for all the economic enterprise of the Municipal Government in order to provide all interested parties of information regarding the performance and profitability of these ventures of the LGU.  Also, the data that will be derived from the financial statements could be used by Management as basis in decision making to monitor operational efficiency and achieve higher income.

We recommended that the municipal officials concerned maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to attain desirable socio-economic developments and environmental outcomes for the municipality and its intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the GAD focal person submit the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to oversight agencies on due date as required under Item C.6.2 and C.6.7 of -DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and Item V of  Circular No. 2014 – 001 to ensure the relevance of the GAD PPAs to the identified gender issues and their causes as well as the correctness and alignment of the entries in the GPB Form.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant check properly the disbursement voucher including the supporting documents before affixing her signature on the voucher to avoid suspensions in post-audit.

Also, the Municipal Accountant should see to it that Purchase Orders bear control numbers and must contain all the relevant data that are required to be indicated in the form.

We have recommended that the Municipal Government maintain separate depository accounts for each fund to facilitate monitoring of available balances and avoid incurrence of unnecessary bank charges and to simplify the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We have recommended that the Management, particularly the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), regularly procure common-use supplies, materials and equipment which are necessary in its operations from the Procurement Service-Department of Budget and Management (PS-DBM) as provided under  Administrative Order No. 17 to contribute to the attainment of a modernized and standardized government procurement system and to avail of the most economic  price in the acquisition of supplies and materials.

We have recommended that the municipality intensify the preparation of its Annual Procurement Plan (APP) by extensively determining the procurement projects that are crucial to the effective discharge of its governmental function or are in pursuit of its principal mandate. By doing so, procurements of supplies as needed in the daily operation of the municipality can be made on a cost-efficient and timely manner and not as the need arises.
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that payments of terminal leave benefits should be supported with complete documentation to avoid suspension in audit.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant use the prescribed account title in the recognition and recording of funds released to Non-Government Organizations/People’s Organizations as set forth by COA Circular No. 2015-009. Compliance to the provisions of the said circular would result to fair presentation of the related items in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant use the prescribed account title in the recognition and recording of payments made for infrastructure projects of the municipality as set forth by COA Circular No. 2015-009. Compliance to the provisions of the said circular would result to fair presentation of the related items in the financial statements.

We have again recommended that the Municipal Treasurer-ICO remit immediately the amount due pursuant to BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98.  Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary documents to facilitate the immediate remittance of the unremitted taxes.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain special accounts under the GF for all the economic enterprise of the Municipal Government in order to provide all interested parties of information regarding the performance and profitability of these ventures of the LGU.  Also, the data that will be derived from the financial statements could be used by Management as basis in decision making to monitor operational efficiency and achieve higher income.

We have recommended that the municipal officials concerned maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to attain desirable socio-economic developments and environmental outcomes for the municipality and its intended beneficiaries.

We have recommended that the GAD focal person submit the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to oversight agencies on due date as required under Item C.6.2 and C.6.7 of -DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and Item V of  Circular No. 2014 – 001 to ensure the relevance of the GAD PPAs to the identified gender issues and their causes as well as the correctness and alignment of the entries in the GPB Form.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant check properly the disbursement voucher including the supporting documents before affixing her signature on the voucher to avoid suspensions in post-audit.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive to require the inventory committee to conduct complete physical  inventory of all the properties of the municipality and to prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RCPPE) and furnish the Auditor a copy for verification.
observations.managementAction: The municipality through the Inventory and Appraisal Committee has started the physical count of the items of PPE and the Reports on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPEs) have been prepared and submitted to the Auditor though those were not complete.
observations.reason: There were noted deficiencies in the submitted RPCPPEs and such were discussed exhaustively in Observation No. 1, page 29-31 of this report.
